rationale_for_baseline_tier: A high-energy head injury with witnessed LOC, post-traumatic amnesia, repeated vomiting, a progressively declining conscious level and a unilateral fixed dilating pupil is an actively herniating intracranial catastrophe. This is an immediate threat to life with a very short window before brainstem compression — T5 (call 000, Priority 1 / intensive-care paramedic, emergency ambulance to a neurosurgical centre) is the only appropriate tier. Any delay risks fatal herniation; airway protection and ICP-aware positioning are the only bystander interventions until paramedics arrive.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Head injury with witnessed loss of consciousness, amnesia/confusion, or more than one episode of vomiting
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 now — do not wait to see if he improves
escalation_edges.rationale: This constellation after significant head trauma mandates immediate emergency assessment and urgent CT; it cannot be safely observed at home.
escalation_edges.communication_template: He was knocked out, he's confused and he's been sick several times after a hard fall on his head — this needs an ambulance now. I'm going to help you call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting in a drowsy patient — aspiration risk
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately, and each time he vomits while the ambulance is en route
escalation_edges.rationale: A vomiting patient with a falling conscious level can aspirate and obstruct the airway within seconds; side positioning with spinal alignment is life-saving.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he's sick again while he's this drowsy, gently roll him onto his side as one unit, keeping his head and neck in line, so he doesn't choke. Keep the airway clear and stay on the line with 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening drowsiness, a new or worsening unequal pupil, a seizure, or any sign the pressure in the head is rising
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — relay changes to 000 as they happen
escalation_edges.rationale: Progression of herniation signs is preterminal; the paramedic crew and receiving neurosurgical team must be forewarned to expedite airway control and surgery.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he gets harder to wake, his eye changes more, he has a fit, or his breathing changes, tell 000 straight away — these mean the pressure in his head is getting worse.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He stops responding or stops breathing normally
escalation_edges.rationale: Respiratory arrest from brainstem compression requires immediate airway management and CPR; the 000 call-taker will give dispatcher-assisted instructions.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he stops breathing normally or you can't wake him at all, tell 000 immediately — they'll talk you through starting CPR while the crew is on the way.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: The only person present is a roommate who is frightened and managing the patient alone
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give one clear instruction at a time, keep the caller on the line, and have them keep the front door unlocked and lights on so paramedics can reach the patient without delay.
Do NOT let a possible alcohol history downgrade the response — the focal neurological signs mandate the same emergency pathway regardless.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 now for an emergency ambulance and tell them it is a serious head injury with an unequal pupil and a person who is getting harder to wake. Keep him lying still with his head and neck supported and slightly raised; do not let him sit up, stand, walk, eat or drink. If he vomits while drowsy, roll him onto his side as one unit keeping the head and neck aligned. Stay with him, keep watching his breathing, and update 000 if anything changes.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately and stay on the line
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is a time-critical emergency and must not wait for any clinic or after-hours service.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Paramedics and the hospital will document the mechanism, the timeline of deterioration, GCS trend, pupil findings and CT results; a discharge/operative summary should follow to the GP once the acute episode is managed.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an actively herniating head injury as a concussion or as intoxication and advising home observation, GP review or 'wake him every few hours'.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The expanding haematoma compresses the brainstem; without emergency CT and neurosurgical evacuation the patient herniates and dies, or survives with catastrophic neurological disability.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising the patient go to an emergency department by self-transport rather than dispatching an emergency ambulance — unmonitored transport of a vomiting, deteriorating patient risks airway obstruction and unattended arrest en route, with no pre-alert to the neurosurgical centre.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a realistic concern in this case — T5 is correct and there is no meaningful higher tier to over-call; the danger here is entirely under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm the bleed, grade the GCS, or measure vitals, and cannot distinguish extradural from subdural or parenchymal haemorrhage.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the pattern, not the missing numbers — the caregiver-reported declining consciousness, repeated vomiting and unequal pupil are sufficient to mandate immediate 000; the exact subtype does not change the emergency action.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the inability to examine or measure as a reason to 'watch and recheck later', or waiting for the patient to worsen further before escalating.
